title: Fast Dynamo Action On The 3-Torus For Pulsed-Diffusions
speaker: Massimo Sorella
abstract: For the passive vector equation, the fast dynamo conjecture predicts exponential-in-time growth of the L2 norm of the solution under the Lipschitz flow generated by a vector field, at a rate independent of the resistivity. We prove this conjecture for the pulsed diffusion model with a time-periodic stretch-fold-shear (SFS) vector field. Our approach relies on anisotropic Banach spaces adapted to the underlying flow dynamics. In the zero-diffusivity regime, we establish the existence of a distributional eigenfunction of the time-one solution operator corresponding to a discrete eigenvalue of modulus greater than one, and then treat the resistive term as a perturbation in these spaces.

proof_stages.label: Pulsed-diffusion model and dynamo theorem
proof_stages.summary: The talk formulates the linear induction equation with alternating ideal transport and heat diffusion, writes the two-step solution operator, defines fast and perfect dynamos, states the existence theorem for a time-periodic divergence-free Lipschitz velocity on the three-torus, and records the main open variants.

proof_stages.label: Obstructions and spectral strategy
proof_stages.summary: Anti-dynamo results and the two-dimensional mixing picture show why diffusion normally destroys stretched fields, motivating a genuinely three-dimensional large-scale separation mechanism and a search for isolated spectrum in a distributional Banach space.

proof_stages.label: Velocity construction and two-dimensional reduction
proof_stages.summary: A one-parameter family of pulsed, time-periodic velocities is defined from horizontal and vertical shear stages, its time-one map and inverse are computed, and the first z-Fourier mode of the magnetic field is reduced to a normalized two-dimensional sheared transfer operator.

proof_stages.label: Heuristic expanding eigenfunction
proof_stages.summary: For large shear strength alpha, a sign function in the expanding coordinate is mapped to the corresponding sign function in the other coordinate up to high-frequency error, and the vertical shear is chosen so that this returns an approximate eigenfunction with eigenvalue of size alpha squared.

proof_stages.label: Anisotropic Banach space and limiting operator
proof_stages.summary: An anisotropic distributional Banach space adapted to the stable and unstable cones controls the high-frequency error, gives convergence of the normalized operator to an explicit rank-one limit, and yields a nonzero isolated eigenvalue under a quadrant-average condition on the shear, proving the perfect dynamo case.

proof_stages.label: Diffusive perturbation and fast dynamo
proof_stages.summary: The heat step is reintroduced using Keller-Liverani perturbation theory: uniform Lasota-Yorke estimates and weak-strong convergence of the heat semigroup preserve the isolated eigenvalue for small epsilon, giving exponential growth independent of epsilon.

examples.name: Two-dimensional anti-dynamo cancellation
examples.note: The two-dimensional potential formulation reduces the magnetic equation to transport-diffusion and exhibits diffusion averaging positive and negative mixed regions, leading directly to the design principle that three-dimensional growth must be separated at large scales.
examples.engagement_reason: derives the two-dimensional cancellation by writing the magnetic field via a scalar potential, reducing to transport-diffusion, estimating norms, and concluding decay rather than growth
